transcript.transcript: My name is Chris Fox. Today is the 10th of April, 1979. I'm going to interview Esther Johnson. The general topic will concern Esther Johnson's father and his settlement on Stanley Hill. Go ahead and go. Edward Stanley came to Port of Wayne with the 2nd Regiment about 1878 And he was stationed at Fort Sherman Then when he's in listen Expired he stayed in the area for a while after cooking in the camps and carrying mail across Lake Pend Oreille and Then he decided that he wanted to stay here. So he wanted to start a little ranch So he bought a little one to buy some land and at that time the railroad every other section of land Across the United States and so he purchased 160 acres What became known as Stanley Hill And that was in 1905 That he bought that from the railroad for $440. That was an awful lot of money at that time. And then My dad came from Sweden in about 1905. And he came out to Spokane. Well, he came to Minneapolis. And then he sent for my mother, who he married in Minneapolis. She was from Denmark. And then they came out to Spokane and He had a Restaurant in Spokane And he traded that restaurant for a little piece of property here, only 22 acres on this hill. And he was also a boarding contractor on the railroad. So He traveled in three states, Idaho, Washington, and Oregon. And then he finally settled down up on the farm here. There was just a little house here to begin with. And then he built this house here about the time of the first world war and then he built the big barn there about the latter part of the war And when he came here this was all timber And he had to clear all this land, and he planted it all into apple trees. And when Mr. Stanley came here, it was all timber, too. And he planted a number of acres into cherry trees. And as time went on, my father bought this land from Mr. Stanley, and we harvested the cherry crop. And we started quite a business by selling extending Selling the cherries into North Dakota And it was kind of a mail-order business and we were selling about 20 tons of cherries every summer. That sounds pretty good. And we were getting about $0.10, $0.12 a pound. Of course, that's pretty cheap now, compared to what they're getting now. But then, about 1926 or 27, it was having pretty nice weather, the fall of the year. And the staff was still up in the trees. And once the temperature dropped about 20 degrees or more, and it seemed to have killed off a lot of the cherry trees. So eventually, we had to pull out all the cherry trees That happened all over all over the whole country here And then just about that time why they will begin shipping cherries in here from Lewiston and brought in the cherry fruit pie. And so we never heard of a worm in a cherry before, until that cherry fruit fly came here. And so ever since then, there's only been worms in the cherries, and they've had to spray. So I guess it was a good thing that That all happened at that time. That must have been quite a project, pulling all those trees out, wasn't it? Oh, that was a big job. That was hard work. That's hard wood, you know? Yeah. Yeah. Some of those trees were 40 years old or more. They had great big jumps on them like that, you know, that big around. I don't know, they must have been 50, 60 feet high. They just cover a great big area. They were immense trees. It sure was fun to pick cherries, though. This was sure a beautiful country. Cherry trees were in blossom in the month of May. And then it was also a beautiful thing to see all the beautiful fruit hanging on the trees Now All the trees are gone, I don't believe there's a single cherry tree on the hill. You must have missed them all. Yeah. 20 minutes. Yeah. Got them all out. There's a few cherry trees down here. A few survivors left. On Ray Jones's property. Yeah, that's about it. And there's some way over there on the sunny side. Otherwise, I think most of the cherry trees are gone. But this whole country used to be more or less a fruit country. On the Hayden Lake and Dalton Gardens and all around there was just all fruit. And it was mainstay, huh? Hm? And it was mainstay. Yeah. And that primary product. And they used to raise lots of vegetables, too, of itself in gardens. And they had a farmer's market in town. And Every Saturday, all the farmers would bring their produce to the market. And at that time, we used to sell apple cider. We still had the apples, and we made 50 gallons of cider. So that was a kind of a farmer's market, huh? Yeah. Was that kind of a festival, too? Hm? Did you have, was that kind of a festival type of a thing? Have dances or anything like that, entertainment of any kind? Or was it just basically just selling the produce? You're selling the produce, uh-huh. Yeah, they even sold meat there in that market. And the people in town came there to buy their things. It was right above where the post office, the old post office was. About where the old post office now is. It's a federal building now, I guess. OK. We used to have barn dances at the big barn. Oh, did you? Uh-huh. We used to have about, we had barn dances there several times. And there were about 300, 400 people there. We had a band. That sounds like a lot of fun. A lot of people still talk about it, about the barn dances. It's kind of nice. A lot of entertainment there, huh? Mm-hmm. Yeah. That was kind of your big entertainment, wasn't it? Something like that. We had to be careful that there was no smoking, you know? We couldn't allow any smoking or drinking. Might be a little too wild. Yeah. More ways than one. Yeah, that's right. What did your father get into after he took the cherry trees out? Well, we kind of planted it into hay land, held out for the grasses. And we had a few cattle. That's not all. Oh, that sounds good. Why don't you get into something about the, maybe your life on that, why don't you talk about your life or just your, just your general living conditions back then, like what you, like your cookie or just things like that. Who, my dad? Yeah, your dad, what they did for... Well, Before he went into the farming, he was on the railroad. He was the boarding contractor on the railroad. And he had several camps all over in these three states. And he was a very good cook himself. So was he. What was life like back then when you were kind of living on a mountainside for you? What? What was life like? Life like living on a mountainside or liking your house under cherry trees? Oh, I liked that cherry. Were you satisfied with that? Yeah, I liked that real well. That was an awful lot of fun. We'd get about 15 or 20 pickers and get them out there in the cherry orchard, you know. And we used to have a lot of fun. And then at the end of the day, my dad would load us all into a truck, All the pickers and anyone that wanted to go and take a zone to the lake and wash off some of the dust that we accumulated in the orchard. We used to haul the cherries. The cherries were put up in 20-pound boxes and were shipped by express to North Dakota. We had mail order business. And we had quite a business there for a while. What kind of a house did you live in? Was it fairly simple kind of a dwelling? What kind of a house? How many rooms were in it? Oh, the house? It was a little house that stood out here, and probably only had two rooms in it. But then my dad moved it, and he added another room onto it. So now it's a little four-room house. But then he built this house. He built this one. You have a house and a barn and a court. That big barn out there. Oh, that's the same one? Yeah, that's the same one. That looks like it's brand new. If we keep it up. Yeah, it does look nice. Mm-hmm. Did you go to school back then? Did they have schoolhouses? Where you would attend or what your mother tutored you? We went to school in town. Oh, did you? Uh-huh. We don't like to go to school. Did you have to walk or? Well, when we were kids, we had a little pony. Oh, did you? A little pony, a little shuttling pony. And we had a little cart. And we were real small, like in the first, second grade, when my mother used to take us in that little pony and cart to school. And we'd go along the streets, and there'd be a whole string of kids following along behind us. And we also had a big dog that used to run along the side of that little pony. And people used to say, my, your dog's almost as big as your horse. Pony wasn't too big, huh? No. But a lot of people still remember that little pony. Yeah, we had a little sleigh for it and a saddle. Speaking about it. My whole life was centered around that little pony. When you were younger? Mm-hmm. Speaking of that sleigh, What was it like during the winter time? What were the winters? We used to have some pretty hard winters here Yeah Not harder than what we had last winter. I Seen it one time when it was 30 30 degrees below zero for about three weeks. And I've seen a lot of times when it's been 30 below. And one time, it even went 45 below. And a lot more snow than what we have now. It's really cold, huh? I sure enjoyed last winter. And with that, we had some snow. What did you do to keep yourself busy during those long, cold winters? I like to go out skiing. I used to do some skiing. And so I got back in practice on my skiing this last winter. Because there's been so many winters there, we didn't have hardly enough snow for skiing. What did you do back when you were younger? Well, I was always busy doing something. Always doing something, right? Yeah, and of course went to school, and of course we had chores to do, I guess. Well, what were some of the chores that you did? Well. Just around the house there in the barn. Just around the house. And high school, the boys had carrying wood and things like that. We were one of the first ones to get electricity. We got Washington Water Power, brought the electric up here. Well, before the Washington Water Power Company, we had a Jinko light plant, an Adelco light plant. And you had to charge up the batteries every so often. Interesting. Did you ever have any problems with that? Well, you know, every once in a while, and It wouldn't run, just like all kinds of engines. We had a great big wooden water tank out here years ago, I remember. And we used to have to pump the water from the well up into that water tank. It was a wooden water tank. Did you have a stove back? Did you have a wood stove back during your childhood? Yeah, we've done wood stove. We've done monarch range. No, I mean back with your father's settlement. What did you use like for heat? To heat the house during your father's? No, I don't think they had electric heat in the old days. Oh, no, not electric. I mean like a wood-burning stove. Did you have that back when you were younger? Yeah, we had a wood stove. You used that to cook on and heat the house? Uh-huh, yeah. Here's the stove I have in the kitchen here. You want to see it? Oh, I don't know what that is. Huh? Mm-hmm. I mean, I saw that on the way in. Yeah, you saw it when you came in? Is that the same stove that you used? Yeah, that's the same one. Uh-huh. Okay. Yeah, that's the same one. Did you hear? Uh-huh. And when my dad built this house, why, his cousin said, oh, You don't need no bathroom on a farm. And my dad says, that's where you need a bathroom, is on a farm. And you come in from the field, and you're all dirty white. That's when you need a bathroom. So we were one of the first ones to have a bathroom on the farm. Was your dad primarily interested in the cherry trees, as far as an occupation goes? Was your dad primarily interested in the cherry trees as far as just agriculture concerning his occupation? Yeah, he was. Just farming. Cherry trees, he liked that. What about, what did he do after the cherry trees were taken out? He just went to farming. Just continued to be farming? Did he have any other activities? Raised hay. Did he have any livestock? He raised 15, 20 ton of hay. We have a few livestock. We still have a few livestock. We still raise hay. OK. Can you recall, like, you may not remember anywhere, Was there any folklore around then? Any what? Folklore or superstitions, anything like that that you can remember back? No. Back then about When you were farming up on the hill here? No, nothing like that. You weren't doing any logger or farming? I know how we were neighbors and friendly with people for miles around. Clear down to Fernand Lake and clear down to 15th Street, we were what you'd call neighbors. New everyone, huh? Uh-huh. Yeah. There wasn't many people on. Yeah, it's different now. We haven't even held hands. Sometimes you don't know your next-door neighbors We We belong to the farmers union we went to meetings all over the country You do a lot of things with your neighbors have a lot of huh? You do a lot of things with your neighbors? Yeah. A lot of activities with them? Well, we both had to visit. We sometimes in the wintertime, why, we'd hitch up to team. And Bob Sladden would go over to spend the day and have dinner with our neighbors that lived way over on Grand Hill. There is a... M-hm. M-hm. I don't know how long it's been. But then, that was in the real early days. Of course, then we got the Model T Ford came along. And Ed Stanley, I think, was one of the first ones to have the Model T Ford. And my dad. And I know it was an all-day trip when they went to Spokane. That was in the Model T. Yeah. And sometimes a Model T couldn't get up the hill. Sometimes they'd have to turn it around and you'd have to come up backwards. Pull itself up on instead of push. Yeah. We had this hill down in front of the house here, why the cars couldn't get up there at all. Too steep. Yeah, the hills are. Yeah. Used to be a big problem in the wintertime to keep the roads plowed out so you could even get into town. Did your dad ever engage in any hunting or anything like that? Hunting? Hunting as far as deer or bear or elk. He wasn't much of a hunter. No, he wasn't much on hunting. Didn't do much in that. Yeah. Primarily just his farming. Way up in these later years, he did kill one deer down here. But he never went out and did any farming. No. He never went out and did any farming. No. Yeah. With Mr. Stanley, he used to raise lots of strawberries, too. Did he? Yeah. And I can still see him in the early morning, walking around, looking out for the birds, the robins, chasing them away out of the strawberry patch. And I used to pick strawberries for them. That was one of the ways you kept busy, huh? That was one of the ways you kept busy, huh? Yeah. Can you think of anything, Freddie? Having fun listening to you. Yeah. It seems like an awful long half hour, isn't it? Yeah, Well, let's see. Do you remember anything specifically about your mother's cooking, the way she would cook, or the method that she used? My mother came from Denmark. So Much of her cooking was along the Danish line. They liked sweet soups, sweet fruit soups. My dad, he was a very good cook. He was especially good at cooking meat and making apple pies. Oh, he was a good cook, too, huh? Yeah. I guess you were well fed, weren't you? Yeah. Well, my dad surely did a lot of wonderful things. He was a wonderful person, my dad and my mother. And my dad, he went back in about 1925 or so, about 1925 or so, or 35, and married my mother's sister. My mother had died. So I brought her over to this country, and that was one of the most wonderful things she did, was to bring her over here. How about the Coeur d'Alene Line? Did you do anything on that? Did you have any activities regarding the water? There's a lot of water around here. Did you? I remember one time we hitched up one of our horses and went in a buggy way off to Silver Beach. Oh, did you? Yeah. That must have been quite a trip for you. Yeah, that was. And, Was there much activity on the lake then? Huh? Was there much activity on the lake? Oh, no. There was no. As far as any steamboats or? Oh, yeah. There used to be steamboats. There used to be a few steamboats on the way? Yeah. I can remember the big steamboats. Mm-hmm. Did you ever sort of took a couple of trips on them? The big steamboats, too, I remember. Mm-hmm. Yeah. Where did you go? Just on the, One up to Harrison. One end of the lake from the other? Up to Harrison and Carlin Bay, I guess. My dad would tell a long time he was on a steamboat up to Harrison and he had a big raccoon coat on and He accidentally stepped off the plank and fell into the lake with that big fur coat on. And they foam out pretty quick. Yeah, it must have been any kind of fun riding around in steamboat weather. Yeah. Were those fairly large vessels? Were those fairly large vessels, those steamboats? Oh, yeah, they were big. Kind of in paddle wheel and stuff. I don't know whether they had paddlewheels. I don't know. There's some pictures of them around. I know there was the Georgie Oaks. I think that's the one that burned. I think they burned it. Yeah, that was a common method. The Mississippi can. What kind of fare did they charge? Huh? What kind of fare did they charge as far as riding the steam? How much did it cost you? Did it cost you anything? I don't know. I haven't any idea. I guess I was too young for to know about that. Okay. Well, I certainly thank you for your time that you've given us. I'm sure this information can be put to some good use.

lines.line: # Introduction by Chris Fox
> Chris Fox introduces the interview by stating his name and today's date. He outlines that the interview will focus on Esther Johnson's father and his settlement on Stanley Hill.
** introduction, Chris Fox, interview overview, Stanley Hill **
keywords: ** introduction, Chris Fox, interview overview, Stanley Hill **

lines.line: # Edward Stanley's Arrival and Settlement

> Discussion on how Edward Stanley came to Fort Wayne with the 2nd Regiment in 1878 and eventually settled on Stanley Hill in 1905 after his enlistment expired. He purchased 160 acres from the railroad.
** Edward Stanley, Fort Wayne, 2nd Regiment, Stanley Hill, land purchase **
keywords: ** Edward Stanley, Fort Wayne, 2nd Regiment, Stanley Hill, land purchase **

metadata.title: Edward Stanley's Arrival and Settlement
metadata.synopsis: Discussion on how Edward Stanley came to Fort Wayne with the 2nd Regiment in 1878 and eventually settled on Stanley Hill in 1905 after his enlistment expired. He purchased 160 acres from the railroad.
metadata.keywords: Edward Stanley, Fort Wayne, 2nd Regiment, Stanley Hill, land purchase

lines.line: # Family Roots: From Sweden and Denmark to Spokane
> Esther talks about her father's arrival from Sweden, her mother's journey from Denmark, and the beginning of their life together in Spokane, including trading a restaurant for property.
** family roots, Sweden, Denmark, Spokane, restaurant trade **
keywords: ** family roots, Sweden, Denmark, Spokane, restaurant trade **

metadata.title: Family Roots: From Sweden and Denmark to Spokane
metadata.synopsis: Esther talks about her father's arrival from Sweden, her mother's journey from Denmark, and the beginning of their life together in Spokane, including trading a restaurant for property.
metadata.keywords: family roots, Sweden, Denmark, Spokane, restaurant trade

lines.line: # Life on the Farm: Building and Agriculture
> Esther describes how her father established their home and farm, clearing timber for apple and cherry orchards, and eventually building a bigger house and barn.
** farm life, construction, apple orchards, cherry orchards, World War I **
keywords: ** farm life, construction, apple orchards, cherry orchards, World War I **

metadata.title: Life on the Farm: Building and Agriculture
metadata.synopsis: Esther describes how her father established their home and farm, clearing timber for apple and cherry orchards, and eventually building a bigger house and barn.

lines.line: # Cherry Business: Growth and Challenges
> The booming cherry business which grew by selling cherries via mail-order to North Dakota, and the eventual downfall due to cold weather and cherry fruit flies.
** cherry business, North Dakota, mail-order, challenges, cherry fruit flies **
keywords: ** cherry business, North Dakota, mail-order, challenges, cherry fruit flies **

lines.line: # Shift in Agriculture and Community Life
> Transition from cherry trees to hay farming, community farmer's market activities including selling cider, and recollections of old community events like barn dances.
** agriculture shift, hay farming, community life, farmer's market, barn dances **
keywords: ** agriculture shift, hay farming, community life, farmer's market, barn dances **

lines.line: # Lifestyle and Daily Routines

> Describes daily life, chores, and the experience of growing up with farm animals and dealing with seasonal changes. Also, talks about their living conditions and how they had one of the first on-farm bathrooms.
** daily routine, chores, seasonal changes, living conditions, on-farm bathroom **
keywords: ** daily routine, chores, seasonal changes, living conditions, on-farm bathroom **

** technology, electricity, transportation, Model T Ford, infrastructure **
